attrs.alt: RBAC vs Azure Policy side-by-side: RBAC controls WHO can act, Policy controls WHAT resources must be

content.text: RBAC and Azure Policy both enforce governance, but they answer fundamentally different questions and should be used together. Relying on only one leaves gaps in your security model.
Azure RBAC answers: who (which identity) can do what (which ARM operation) on which resource? RBAC controls the ability to act.
Azure Policy answers: what configuration must resources have? Policy controls the state of resources, regardless of who created them.
A developer with Contributor role can create a storage account — RBAC allows it. But if a policy requires HTTPS-only and the developer sets allowBlobPublicAccess=true, Policy blocks or flags it.
RBAC and Policy are evaluated independently; both must permit a request for it to succeed.
RBAC: controls the door (can you enter?). Policy: controls the room (what must be inside?). You need both for a complete governance model.
The resource being created or modified
Default deny — no role = no access
Default allow — no policy = no restriction
Audit, Deny, Modify, Append, DeployIfNotExists, AuditIfNotExists
Policy definitions specify an effect that determines what happens when a resource matches the policy condition. Effects range from read-only auditing to active blocking and automatic remediation.
Hard compliance requirements (allowed regions, required encryption)
Allows the operation but marks resource as non-compliant
Adds or replaces resource tags or properties at create/update time
Auto-apply tags, enforce settings on new resources
Adds fields to the resource request before it is processed
Inject required properties not set by the deployer
Deploys a linked resource when the condition is not met
Audits resources that lack a related resource
Flag VMs without backup policy, storage without diagnostics
Temporarily disable a policy without deleting the assignment

DeployIfNotExists (DINE) is the most powerful Policy effect. It triggers an ARM deployment when the policy condition is met but the expected related resource is absent. DINE policies require a managed identity with appropriate RBAC permissions to perform the deployment.
The policy assignment's managed identity must have the RBAC roles listed in roleDefinitionIds at the assignment scope.
Remediation can be triggered manually (az policy remediation create) or automatically when a non-compliant resource is detected.
Existing non-compliant resources are not automatically remediated at assignment time — you must trigger a remediation task.
DINE deployments run asynchronously and appear as ARM deployments in the resource group's deployment history.

The most robust governance designs assign both: RBAC grants teams the ability to perform operations, and Policy constrains the configuration of resources those operations produce. Neither alone is sufficient.

# Real-world pattern: RBAC + Policy together
# Scenario: Dev team should be able to create VMs, but only in allowed regions
# and only with approved SKUs.

# 1. RBAC: grant the dev team permission to create VMs
New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId          (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'dev-team').Id `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Contributor' `
    -Scope             '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/dev-rg'

# 2. Policy: restrict VM SKUs to approved sizes only
$policyDef = Get-AzPolicyDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.Properties.DisplayName -eq 'Allowed virtual machine size SKUs'
}
New-AzPolicyAssignment `
    -Name             'allowed-vm-skus' `
    -Scope            '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/dev-rg' `
    -PolicyDefinition $policyDef `
    -PolicyParameterObject @{ listOfAllowedSKUs = @{ value = @('Standard_D2s_v3','Standard_D4s_v3') } }

# Result: dev team can create VMs (RBAC allows it),
# but ARM blocks any VM not in the approved SKU list (Policy denies it).
# BOTH constraints must be satisfied for a successful deployment.
Prevent a user from deleting VMs
RBAC is the right tool for identity-based restrictions
Require all resources to have a CostCenter tag
Allow only specific resource types in a subscription
Policy governs what can be created, not who creates it
Grant read access to a production storage account
Block public IP creation in a dev subscription
Policy blocks the resource regardless of who tries
Enforce diagnostic settings on all new VMs

An initiative (policy set) bundles multiple policy definitions into a single assignment. Microsoft provides built-in initiatives for all major compliance frameworks, making it straightforward to assess and enforce baseline compliance.

# Policy initiatives (policy sets) group multiple policies under one assignment
# Microsoft provides built-in initiatives for major compliance frameworks:
#   - Azure Security Benchmark
#   - CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations Benchmark
#   - NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5
#   - PCI DSS v4.0

# Assign the Azure Security Benchmark initiative:
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'asb-assignment' \
  --policy-set-definition '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

# Check the number of non-compliant resources after assignment:
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' \
  --policy-assignment 'asb-assignment' \
  --query 'results.nonCompliantResources'
Azure Security Benchmark: Microsoft's own baseline, mapped to CIS Controls. Recommended starting point for all Azure environments.
CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations Benchmark: industry-recognized hardening guidance for Azure.
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: US federal standard, widely required for government and regulated industries.
PCI DSS: required for environments that process payment card data.
Each initiative assignment creates a compliance dashboard in the Azure Policy portal showing resource-level compliance state.
When a resource legitimately cannot comply with a policy, an exemption excludes it from evaluation without removing the policy assignment from the broader scope.

# Policy exemptions allow specific resources or scopes to bypass a policy
# without removing the policy assignment entirely.
# Two categories:
#   Waiver:     resource is genuinely exempt from the requirement
#   Mitigated:  resource is compliant through an alternative control

# Create a policy exemption for a specific storage account
New-AzPolicyExemption `
    -Name             'legacy-sa-exemption' `
    -Scope            '/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/legacysa' `
    -PolicyAssignmentId '/subscriptions/.../providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/require-https-storage' `
    -ExemptionCategory 'Waiver' `
    -Description      'Legacy storage account does not support HTTPS; decommission by Q3' `
    -ExpiresOn        (Get-Date '2025-09-30')

# Exemptions inherit downward like assignments.
# An exemption at the resource group scope exempts ALL resources in the group.
# Always set an expiry date — open-ended exemptions become permanent compliance gaps.
Treat exemptions as technical debt. Always set an expiry date and attach a tracking ticket. Exemptions without expiry dates accumulate into permanent compliance gaps that auditors will flag.

Use RBAC when the governance question involves who can perform an action. Examples: read-only access for auditors, write access for a deployment service principal, preventing users from assigning roles.
Use Policy when the governance question involves what a resource must look like. Examples: required tags, approved regions, mandatory encryption settings, allowed VM SKUs.
Use both when you need to control both access and configuration: RBAC permits the team to deploy resources; Policy ensures every resource they deploy meets compliance requirements.

Never try to simulate Policy with RBAC by removing specific resource-type actions from roles. This doesn't scale — every new resource type needs a NotActions entry, and it doesn't remediate existing non-compliant resources.
Never try to simulate RBAC with Policy. Policy cannot grant access to specific users; a Deny policy blocks everyone including admins (unless they are excluded in the policy rule).
Managing Policy with Infrastructure as Code
Policy definitions and assignments should be version-controlled alongside the infrastructure they govern. Both Bicep and Terraform provide first-class support for policy resources.
Store policy definitions in a central platform repository, not in application team repositories. This ensures consistent enforcement across subscriptions.
Use parameter files to deploy the same policy definition with different parameter values across dev, staging, and production subscriptions.
Policy assignments that use DeployIfNotExists require an identity block in Bicep or a managed identity configured on the azurerm_subscription_policy_assignment Terraform resource.
Test policy effects in Audit mode in dev/staging before switching to Deny in production.

A newly assigned policy does not retroactively fix existing resources. Policy evaluates new or updated resources on creation and evaluates existing resources on a compliance scan (triggered automatically every 24 hours or manually). For Modify and DINE policies, you must create a remediation task to apply the effect to existing resources.

# Trigger manual remediation for existing non-compliant resources
# After assigning a Modify or DINE policy, existing resources are not
# automatically remediated — you must create a remediation task.

# List non-compliant resources for an assignment
az policy state list \
  --subscription '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' \
  --policy-assignment 'diag-settings' \
  --filter 'complianceState eq NonCompliant' \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId, State:complianceState}' \
  --output table

# Create a remediation task (applies DINE/Modify to non-compliant resources)
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'remediate-diag-settings' \
  --policy-assignment 'diag-settings' \
  --subscription '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# Monitor remediation task progress
az policy remediation show \
  --name 'remediate-diag-settings' \
  --subscription '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' \
  --query '{Status:provisioningState, Succeeded:deploymentStatus.successfulDeployments, Failed:deploymentStatus.failedDeployments}'

Effective governance reporting combines RBAC access data with Policy compliance data. Resources that are both over-privileged (excessive RBAC) and out of compliance (Policy violations) represent the highest risk and should be prioritized in access reviews.

# Audit both RBAC and Policy compliance from a single script
# Useful for quarterly access reviews and compliance reporting
$sub = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# --- RBAC audit ---
Write-Host '=== Role Assignments with broad scope ===' -ForegroundColor Cyan
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope "/subscriptions/$sub" | Where-Object {
    $_.Scope -eq "/subscriptions/$sub" -and
    $_.RoleDefinitionName -in @('Owner','Contributor','User Access Administrator')
} | Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, ObjectType

# --- Policy compliance audit ---
Write-Host '=== Non-compliant policy assignments ===' -ForegroundColor Cyan
az policy state summarize `
  --subscription $sub `
  --query 'policyAssignments[?results.nonCompliantResources>`0`].{Assignment:policyAssignmentId,NonCompliant:results.nonCompliantResources}' `
  --output table

# --- Combined report: resources with both owner RBAC and policy non-compliance ---
# This flags configurations that are both over-privileged AND out of compliance.
Write-Host '=== Owners in non-compliant resource groups ===' -ForegroundColor Yellow
$ncRGs = (az policy state list --subscription $sub --filter 'complianceState eq NonCompliant' `
          --query '[].resourceGroup' --output tsv | Sort-Object -Unique)
foreach ($rg in $ncRGs) {
    Get-AzRoleAssignment -ResourceGroupName $rg | Where-Object { $_.RoleDefinitionName -eq 'Owner' } |
        Select-Object @{n='RG';e={$rg}}, DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName
}

Microsoft Defender for Cloud's 'Secure Score' aggregates both RBAC (over-provisioned identities) and Policy (resource misconfigurations) findings into a single prioritized recommendations list. Enable it as a starting point for combined governance.
RBAC controls who can perform actions on resources; Policy controls what configuration resources must have. They are complementary, not substitutes.
RBAC defaults to deny — no role means no access. Policy defaults to allow — no policy means no restriction.
Policy effects include Audit (non-blocking), Deny (blocking), Modify (auto-correct), and DeployIfNotExists (auto-remediate).
DINE and Modify policies require a managed identity on the policy assignment with appropriate RBAC roles to perform their deployments.
Use initiatives to assign multiple related policies in a single operation and track compliance against a framework.
Use exemptions for legitimate exceptions — always with an expiry date and a tracking justification.
The recommended pattern: RBAC grants teams permissions; Policy governs the resources those teams create.
